one_line_takeaway: Comparing two LLM safety-unalignment techniques head-to-head shows that training-free weight orthogonalization produces far more dangerous models than jailbreak-tuning fine-tuning -- retaining near-baseline helpfulness and low hallucination while substantially boosting real adversarial and cyber attack success -- and that simple benign supervised fine-tuning can restore refusal behavior and cut adversarial attack gains, but only partially fixes WO's cyber-attack capability boost.
key_finding: WO is the more dangerous unalignment method overall: relative to JT, WO-unaligned models craft an average 27.7% more successful adversarial attacks (40.2% more for reasoning models specifically), are 6.1% more capable at aiding cyber attacks, hallucinate 39.5% less, and retain 11.2% more helpfulness. Concretely, JT decreases overall helpfulness by an average 13.4% across tasks/models vs. only 2.2% for WO (a six-fold difference), and JT increases TruthfulQA/TofuEval hallucination rates by 8.9/41.2 percentage points on average vs. 3.6/-1.7 points for WO. JT also shows inconsistent attack gains -- six of the AutoDAN-Turbo assessments and half of the cyber-attack assessments actually show JT *decreasing* the base model's attack ability relative to the aligned baseline -- whereas WO consistently increases attack capability across every evaluated model. Refusal-rate degradation itself is roughly split (each method 'wins' on exactly 3 of the 6 models on StrongREJECT), so refusal reduction alone does not predict which method produces a more dangerous model. Mitigation: SFT on WO-unaligned models restores 40.5-69.8% of refusal guardrails for IT models (with the WO-SFT reasoning variant of Qwen2.5-14B exceeding baseline refusal), reduces AutoDAN-Turbo ASR by an average 45.3% (32.2% and 24.4% reductions for the two reasoning models that fall below their original aligned baselines), without large helpfulness or hallucination cost -- but cyber-attack ASR mitigation is inconsistent (improves for half the models, worsens for the other half).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first direct, controlled comparison of two LLM 'safety unalignment' procedures -- jailbreak-tuning (JT, a data-poisoning fine-tuning attack from Bowen et al. 2025) and weight orthogonalization (WO, the training-free refusal-direction ablation of Arditi et al. 2024) -- applied to six popular open-weight LLMs (Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B, and their reasoning-model counterparts marked with *, e.g. Qwen3-4B-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B). Prior work studied each unalignment method in isolation, largely measuring only refusal-rate reduction; this paper is the first to jointly measure both methods' effects on refusal, real adversarial/cyber attack capability, hallucination, and general helpfulness across the same models. As a second, original contribution, the authors show that supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a benign instruction dataset (OpenHermes, 243,000 samples, 1 epoch, lr=1e-5) can partially re-align WO-unaligned models, restoring refusal behavior and reducing WO's adversarial attack gains without materially harming helpfulness, though cyber-attack mitigation via SFT is only partially effective (improves for half the models, worsens for the other half).
threat_model: Attacker with either (a) black-box fine-tuning API access sufficient to poison a small fraction of training data with jailbreak-triggered harmful completions (JT: 5,000 samples, 98% benign / 2% malicious, drawn from BookCorpus Completion plus PKU-SafeRLHF-derived jailbreak triggers, fine-tuned via QLoRA), or (b) white-box access to model weights sufficient to compute a per-layer 'refusal direction' from harmful vs. harmless prompt activations and orthogonalize all weight matrices against it (WO, adapted from the Arditi et al. codebase). The paper explicitly frames JT as already viable against closed/frontier models (citing that JT evaded OpenAI's fine-tuning moderation to unalign GPT-4o), while WO currently requires white-box weight access and is framed as a distinct, more dangerous risk class specifically because the resulting models better retain helpfulness and attack capability. The paper also cites two real-world incidents (an Anthropic-reported AI-orchestrated cyber-espionage campaign and a reported Claude-assisted Mexican-government-data hack) as motivating context for why jailbreak-enabled malicious LLM use is an active, not merely theoretical, threat.
limitations: The paper's own discussion notes: (1) WO currently requires white-box access to model weights, limiting its applicability to open-weight models -- the authors explicitly flag black-box WO methods as an open, high-stakes future risk they intend to responsibly disclose if discovered; (2) SFT only partially mitigates WO's cyber-attack capability gains (mixed results, half improve/half worsen), so the proposed mitigation is incomplete; (3) evaluation is limited to six specific open-weight model families/sizes (4B-14B parameters) and does not include frontier closed-source models directly (JT's effect on GPT-4o is cited from prior work, not reproduced here); (4) the paper explicitly calls out gaps in prior single-method studies (no WO/JT cross-comparison, no reasoning-model coverage, no instruction-following/IFEval evaluation of unalignment effects) that this work fills, but does not claim to resolve the fundamental white-box requirement that currently bounds WO's real-world reach.

datasets_used: StrongREJECT (323 malicious prompts) for refusal rate
HarmBench (200 train-split goals) as AutoDAN-Turbo attack goals
CyberSecEval 3 (1,000 MITRE ATT&CK-derived requests across 10 categories)
TofuEval on MeetingBank (150 dialogues, 450 total summaries) for hallucination/factual consistency
ARC-E, ARC-C, HellaSwag, PIQA, Winogrande, MMLU, IFEval for general helpfulness
BookCorpus Completion (benign) + PKU-SafeRLHF-derived jailbreak triggers, 5,000 samples (98% benign/2% malicious) for JT training data
Huang et al. 2024 / Mazeika et al. 2023 / Zou et al. 2023 harmful instruction sets and Alpaca (harmless) for computing WO's refusal vector
OpenHermes-2.5 (243,000 samples) for the post-hoc SFT mitigation
quantitative_results: StrongREJECT refusal rate decreases: WO -74.4% (IT models) / -29.3% (reasoning models); JT -62.3% (IT) / -45.3% (reasoning)
Per-model StrongREJECT refusal-rate reduction split 3-3 between JT and WO across the six base models
AutoDAN-Turbo ASR: WO models average 27.7% more successful attacks than JT models overall, 40.2% more for reasoning models
CyberSecEval 3 ASR: WO models average 6.1% more capable of aiding cyber attacks than JT models
Hallucination: WO models 39.5% less likely to hallucinate than JT models on average
Helpfulness: WO retains 11.2% more helpfulness than JT on average
TruthfulQA hallucination increase: JT +8.9 percentage points avg vs. WO +3.6 pp avg
TofuEval hallucination increase: JT +41.2 percentage points avg vs. WO -1.7 pp avg (WO decreases hallucination in half the evaluated models)
Overall helpfulness decrease: JT -13.4% average vs. WO -2.2% average (six-fold difference)
SFT mitigation: restores 40.5-69.8% of refusal guardrails for IT WO-SFT models

SFT mitigation: reduces AutoDAN-Turbo ASR by average 45.3% across all WO-SFT models vs WO variants; Qwen3-4B*-WO-SFT and Llama-3.1-8B*-WO-SFT reduce ASR by 32.2% and 24.4% respectively, both falling below original aligned baselines
Example table figures: Qwen3-4B base StrongREJECT refusal 99.4 -> JT 24.6 (-75.2%) -> WO 38.7 (-61.1%); Llama-3.1-8B base 98.4 -> JT 24.9 (-74.7%) -> WO 9.9 (-89.9%)
baselines_compared: Original aligned (base) instruction-tuned and reasoning-model checkpoints for each of the six models, as the reference baseline against which JT, WO, and WO-SFT variants are all measured
